Name: info_theoretic_compression
Title: Information-Theoretic Limits of Model Compression: A Rate-Distortion Approach
Short Hypothesis: The fundamental limit of model compression is determined by the rate-distortion tradeoff, which can be characterized using information-theoretic measures such as entropy and mutual information.
Related Work: Recent works such as EMQ, AMP-ViT, and SNIP have explored mixed-precision quantization and adaptive precision training for efficient model compression. However, these approaches often rely on heuristic-based methods and lack a rigorous information-theoretic framework. In contrast, our proposal seeks to establish a theoretical foundation for model compression using rate-distortion theory, which can provide a principled approach to optimizing compression ratios while preserving model accuracy.
Abstract: This proposal explores the information-theoretic limits of model compression using a rate-distortion approach. We aim to characterize the fundamental tradeoff between compression ratio and model accuracy, and to develop novel compression algorithms that achieve optimal performance. Our approach will leverage tools from rate-distortion theory, such as entropy and mutual information, to analyze the compressibility of deep neural networks. We will also investigate the role of noise and distortion in model compression, and develop methods to optimize compression ratios while preserving model accuracy.
Experiments: We will conduct experiments to validate our theoretical framework and to evaluate the performance of our compression algorithms. Specifically, we will: (1) analyze the rate-distortion tradeoff for various deep neural networks, (2) develop and evaluate novel compression algorithms that optimize compression ratios while preserving model accuracy, and (3) investigate the role of noise and distortion in model compression. We will use metrics such as entropy, mutual information, and distortion to evaluate the performance of our algorithms.
Risk Factors and Limitations: One potential risk factor is that our theoretical framework may not accurately capture the complexities of real-world model compression scenarios. Additionally, our compression algorithms may not be efficient or scalable for large-scale models. To mitigate these risks, we will conduct thorough experiments and evaluations, and will refine our framework and algorithms based on the results.
